response: Blockers: (1) Business logic in the controller — password hashing and Prisma queries belong in a service; controllers only validate input, delegate, and shape the response. (2) `@Body() body: any` skips DTO validation entirely. Add a `CreateUserDto` with class-validator decorators and rely on the global `ValidationPipe` (`whitelist: true`, `forbidNonWhitelisted: true`, `transform: true`) so unknown fields and bad types are rejected at the boundary. (3) Direct DB access from the controller violates module boundaries; the controller must call a service which calls a repository. (4) The error contract is wrong: returning `200` with `{ success: false }` for a duplicate email hides the failure from clients and triggers no error path. The correct response is HTTP `409` with `{ code: 'DUPLICATE_EMAIL', message, traceId }`, thrown as a typed `ConflictException` and shaped by the global exception filter. Suggestions (non-blocking): rename variables for clarity, extract password-hashing to a small `PasswordHasher` provider for testability, add Swagger decorators on the DTO, and add a unit test for the duplicate path plus an e2e test for the controller. Blockers must be fixed before merge; suggestions can land in a follow-up. See `04-code-quality.md`, `09-validation.md`, `07-standard-responses.md`, and `10-error-handling.md`.

Use cursor pagination for `GET /v1/payments` because payments are append-mostly, growing, and clients usually browse sequentially — cursors stay consistent under inserts and avoid deep-offset DB pain. The endpoint takes a `ListPaymentsQueryDto` validated by class-validator and the global `ValidationPipe`: `cursor?: string`, `limit?: number` (default 20, max 100), `filter[status]` constrained to an enum (`pending|succeeded|failed|refunded`), and `sort` whitelisted to `createdAt`, `-createdAt`, `amount`, `-amount`. Anything outside the whitelist is rejected — never trust the client to pick column names. Repository decodes the cursor into `(createdAt, id)`, applies `WHERE tenant_id = ?` plus the status filter, applies the sort, and selects `limit + 1` to detect `hasMore`. Response uses the standard list contract `{ data: Payment[], meta: { nextCursor, hasMore, limit } }` so every list endpoint shares one client-side handler. Errors stay on the standard `{ code, message, details?, traceId }` shape. Document the DTO with `@ApiProperty` so Swagger renders the exact filter/sort whitelist. Cite `06-api-design.md`, `07-standard-responses.md`, `08-pagination-filters-sorting.md`, and `09-validation.md`.

Only borrow parts. This skill is NestJS-first: it assumes Nest primitives like `@Module`, controllers, providers, guards, pipes, and DTOs. Plain Express has none of those, so do not force Nest-specific decorators, `@Injectable()`-style DI, or `src/{core,common,modules}` folder structure onto the codebase — that creates a half-Nest hybrid that is harder to reason about than either pure framework. What does transfer is the cross-cutting backend guidance: centralized request validation at the boundary (Zod or Joi schemas instead of class-validator DTOs), stable response contracts (`{ data, meta }` for lists; `{ code, message, details?, traceId }` for errors), structured redacted logging with a correlation id per request, server-derived auth identity (never trusted from the body), repository-level tenant filtering, idempotency on money-moving routes, pagination on every list, forward-only migrations, and test-at-boundaries discipline. Apply those outcomes using the existing Express idioms (router-level middleware, service modules, whatever the repo already does). Only escalate to a NestJS migration if there is an independent business reason — do not rewrite an endpoint into Nest just because this skill is loaded. The non-negotiables protect properties; the framework is implementation detail. See the Scope and precedence section of `SKILL.md`.

Respect the repo's equivalent-safe conventions and do not rewrite them mid-PR. The skill's non-negotiables protect outcomes (validated input at the boundary, structured redacted logs, stable contracts), not specific libraries. Zod parsing at the controller boundary is functionally equivalent to class-validator DTOs as long as it rejects unknown fields, coerces types safely, and produces a typed value — keep using Zod and apply the same rules: whitelist mode, no `passthrough`, schemas live next to the route, and they generate the TypeScript type. `pino-http` with redaction (`authorization`, `cookie`, `set-cookie`, `password`, `token`) and a per-request correlation id satisfies the structured-logging non-negotiable just as well as `nestjs-pino`. `src/features` is a healthy alternative to `src/modules` — leave it. In review, only flag things that break the underlying property: a Zod schema in `passthrough` mode, a route that bypasses parsing, missing redaction, or a feature folder importing another feature's repository directly. Escalate harmful conflicts (security weakening, contract instability, tenant-scope leaks) but do not demand a migration to class-validator or `nestjs-pino` for stylistic alignment. Cite `SKILL.md` Scope and precedence and `09-validation.md`.

Build it on three layers. (1) An `LlmGateway` service abstracts providers behind one interface (`stream(input, opts) => AsyncIterable<Chunk>`), handling retry, timeout, fallback, and provider selection — the controller never sees OpenAI/Anthropic directly. (2) Expose a cancel-aware SSE endpoint: `GET /v1/chat/stream` returns `Content-Type: text/event-stream`, `Cache-Control: no-cache`, `Connection: keep-alive`, with periodic heartbeat comments to defeat idle proxies. The handler iterates the gateway stream, writes `data:` events for partial tokens, listens to `req.on('close')` to abort the upstream provider call, and emits a final `event: done` with usage. (3) On every call, write a row to `llm_usage` with `{ user_id, org_id, model, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, total_cost_usd, latency_ms, request_id }` inside the same transaction as the result. Aggregate per user/org/model for quota enforcement and billing; reject with `429` when the tier quota is exceeded. Observability: OpenTelemetry spans wrap the request, the gateway, the provider HTTP call, and DB writes; ship LLM-specific traces to Langfuse or Helicone (prompt, completion, tokens, cost). Structured logs carry `correlation_id` and `request_id`. Cite `26-ai-product-patterns.md`, `27-ai-streaming-sse.md`, `28-ai-usage-metering-cost.md`, and `22-observability.md`.

Not safe. The auth guard proves the caller has a valid session, but it does not constrain which rows they can read. As written, anyone with any valid token can fetch any invoice by guessing or enumerating ids — classic IDOR plus a cross-tenant leak. The repository must scope the query by tenant, derived from the authenticated request context (`request.user.tenantId`), not from the URL or body. Change the signature to `findInvoice(id: string, tenantId: string)` and the SQL to `SELECT * FROM invoices WHERE id = $1 AND tenant_id = $2` (or use a `withTenant(tenantId)` repository wrapper). Tenant scoping must live at the repository layer; do not rely on the service or controller remembering to add the predicate — that is exactly how leaks ship. Layer defense in depth: JwtAuthGuard attaches the tenant id to the request, a TenantGuard rejects if it is missing, services pass it explicitly, and the repository refuses to run without it. Add an integration test that proves tenant A cannot read tenant B's invoice even with a valid JWT. Cite `33-multi-tenancy-patterns.md`, `11-security.md`, and the non-negotiable that every URL id is checked against the authenticated user/org's ownership.

Decide on five things before turning the cache on. (1) Read frequency and origin cost — if `/pricing/:tier` is hit thousands of times per minute and the underlying query is cheap, the cache may not be worth the complexity; (2) staleness tolerance — pricing tiers change rarely but a stale price can mis-charge a customer, so define an explicit max staleness; (3) a clear invalidation path — when an admin updates a tier, the cache must be busted on write; (4) metrics — hit ratio, miss latency, and stale-served counts before and after; (5) authority — the cache must never be the sole truth for a billing/quota decision. Concretely: key `pricing:tier:v1:<tier>` (versioned namespace so a schema change rotates keys), TTL 5 minutes plus event-driven invalidation on `pricing.updated`, and stampede protection via single-flight (one request fills the key, others wait) or a small jittered early-refresh window so a hot key does not stampede the DB on expiry. Say no when pricing is user-specific, contract-negotiated, or feeds a charge/refund decision — at the moment of billing always re-read the source of truth. Cite `24a-caching-patterns.md` and `24-performance.md`.

Split the two probes. Liveness `/healthz/live` is cheap and in-process — it returns 200 if the event loop is responsive and never touches Postgres or Redis; if it fails, Kubernetes restarts the pod. Readiness `/healthz/ready` is gated: returns 200 only after config validation, migrations applied, Postgres reachable (`SELECT 1`), and BullMQ/Redis reachable; on failure, Kubernetes removes the pod from the Service so the load balancer drains it. One shutdown coordinator owns the sequence on `SIGTERM`: (1) flip readiness to false immediately so the LB stops sending new traffic, (2) wait `terminationGracePeriodSeconds` for in-flight requests to drain, (3) `app.close()` to stop accepting HTTP, (4) tell BullMQ workers to stop fetching new jobs and wait for current jobs to finish (`worker.close()`), (5) close the Redis connection, (6) drain the Postgres pool. Each step has a timeout so a stuck dependency does not block forever. BullMQ workers run in their own process and need their own drain; handlers must remain idempotent because Kubernetes can kill mid-job and a retry will replay. Use `enableShutdownHooks()` and `@nestjs/terminus`. Cite `34-health-shutdown.md` and `19-background-jobs.md`.

I will not give pinned versions or install commands from memory — those are volatile facts and recalling them risks staleness. The right answer is a process: verify against the official NestJS docs (docs.nestjs.com), the Node.js LTS schedule (nodejs.org/en/about/previous-releases), and the repo itself — `package.json` for declared ranges, the lockfile (`package-lock.json` / `pnpm-lock.yaml` / `yarn.lock`) for what is actually installed, the Dockerfile for the runtime base image, and the CI workflow for the build/test Node version. The durable advice does not need a lookup: keep runtime, build, test, and Docker Node versions aligned (one pinned LTS major across all of them); validate env at boot with Zod so missing config fails fast; and split modernization PRs from feature PRs so a regression is bisectable. Pin the Dockerfile to a specific minor (`node:<lts>-alpine` is fine for prod, but pin a minor for reproducibility) and let Renovate/Dependabot bump it. Once you confirm the current LTS major and the Nest major from the docs, write them down in the repo's `README` or `docs/runtime.md` so the next teammate has the source of truth without re-asking. Cite `35-source-of-truth-freshness.md` and `32-modern-nestjs-stack.md`.

Three statuses, three error codes, one shape. Validation/unknown-field rejection from `ValidationPipe` returns `422` with `{ code: 'VALIDATION.FAILED', message: 'Request validation failed.', details: [{ field, constraints }], traceId }` — produced by an `exceptionFactory` that throws `UnprocessableEntityException` so the status cannot drift back to 400. A duplicate invoice number — a domain conflict — returns `409` with `{ code: 'INVOICE.DUPLICATE_NUMBER', message: 'Invoice number already exists for this organization', details: { invoiceNumber }, traceId }`. Unexpected system errors return `500` with `{ code: 'INTERNAL.UNEXPECTED', message: 'Something went wrong', traceId }` and the real cause is logged but never leaked. Implementation: a global `ExceptionFilter` shapes every error to that contract; the service throws a typed `DuplicateInvoiceNumberError extends ConflictException` so the controller stays thin and tests assert on the typed class. `traceId` (the request correlation id) is always included so support can pivot from a customer ticket to logs/traces by one id. Validation errors describe the bad input (422 — well-formed JSON, semantically wrong); domain errors describe a business rule (409 — well-formed but conflicts with state); infrastructure errors are opaque to the client. Codes follow the dotted `MODULE.REASON` convention (`VALIDATION.FAILED`, `INVOICE.DUPLICATE_NUMBER`, `INTERNAL.UNEXPECTED`) so clients can branch without parsing strings. Cite `07-standard-responses.md`, `10-error-handling.md`, and `39-exception-filters.md`.

Issues: (1) `@Body() body: any` — no DTO, no validation, accepts anything (NoSQL/JSON injection risk, mass-assignment); replace with `LoginDto` validated by `ValidationPipe` (`whitelist`, `forbidNonWhitelisted`, `transform`). (2) No rate limit — endpoint is brute-forceable; add `@Throttle()` per-IP and per-account, plus exponential lockout after N failed attempts. (3) Timing attack — `password.verify` against a possibly-null `user.password` short-circuits when the email does not exist, leaking which emails are registered; always run the same-cost password verification path against a dummy hash when the user is missing, and use a constant-time bcrypt/argon2 verify primitive. (4) Returning the full `user` row leaks password hashes and PII; return a safe DTO and a session token only. (5) No structured authentication — no session/JWT issuance, no audit log of attempt + IP + UA. (6) Likely logs the body somewhere upstream; redact `password`. OWASP A07 (Identification and Authentication Failures) and A03 (Injection) directly apply. Correct order of checks: DTO validation → rate-limit/lockout check → look up user by email → same-cost password verification using a dummy hash when user is missing → record audit event → issue a session/JWT and store only hashed session/refresh tokens server-side. Cite `11-security.md`, `12-authentication-patterns.md`, and `09-validation.md`.

For a browser web frontend, prefer an opaque session cookie with `httpOnly`, `secure`, and a SameSite value that matches the deployment topology: `lax` or `strict` for same-site apps, `none; secure` only for a true cross-site browser app. `httpOnly` keeps the token out of `document.cookie`, which a `localStorage` Bearer token cannot guarantee. Pair any cookie-authenticated mutation with CSRF protection: double-submit/synchronizer token plus Origin/Fetch Metadata checks as defense in depth. Store only hashed/HMACed session and refresh tokens server-side; the raw token is a bearer secret. For a mobile app there is no browser cookie jar and no CSRF surface, so use a Bearer token in the `Authorization` header, store refresh in the OS secure keychain, and rotate refresh tokens. When the same API serves both, support both schemes and resolve precedence consistently — if both a cookie and a Bearer token are present, the cookie wins, and if that cookie is invalid the request fails closed instead of falling back to Bearer. Refresh strategy: short-lived access token, rotating refresh token, server-side revocation via token family or `revoked_before`. Cite `12-authentication-patterns.md` and `11-security.md`.

Tables are plural nouns in snake_case: `users`, `organizations`, `invoices`, `invoice_line_items`. Primary keys are always `id` — UUIDv7 (time-sortable) for distributed inserts or `bigint` identity for single-writer stores. Foreign keys follow `<entity>_id`: `users.organization_id`, `invoices.organization_id`, `invoices.created_by_user_id`, `invoice_line_items.invoice_id`. Columns are snake_case (`created_at`, `updated_at`, `due_date`, `total_cents`, `currency_code`); money is stored as integer minor units, never floats. Required indexes: every FK gets one (`idx_invoices_organization_id`, `idx_invoice_line_items_invoice_id`); composite indexes on common query paths (`(organization_id, created_at desc)` for tenant-scoped listings, `(organization_id, status)` for status filters, unique `(organization_id, invoice_number)` to enforce per-tenant uniqueness). Soft delete via a nullable `deleted_at timestamptz`; partial indexes use `WHERE deleted_at IS NULL` to keep hot queries fast. Map snake_case columns to camelCase in DTOs/entities at the ORM/serialization layer — DB convention wins because raw SQL and tooling expect it; code convention follows TypeScript norms. Add `created_at`/`updated_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()` everywhere. Cite `13-database-design.md` and `02-naming-conventions.md`.

Default to Prisma or Drizzle. Prisma gives the strongest TypeScript ergonomics — generated types, an excellent migration tool, and a query builder that handles 90% of CRUD safely; the trade-off is less control over complex SQL (raw `$queryRaw` exists but loses type safety) and a separate query engine binary. Drizzle is a thinner SQL-shaped query builder with first-class TypeScript inference and direct access to the underlying driver; better when you need complex joins, CTEs, window functions, or the team prefers writing close-to-SQL. Raw `pg` is correct only when you need maximal control or are building a low-level repository layer; you accept hand-rolling type safety and a lot of boilerplate. TypeORM works but carries decorator-heavy ceremony, lazy-loading footguns, and migration quirks; new services should not start there. Critical point for multi-tenant isolation: no ORM enforces tenant scoping for you. Every repository method must explicitly include `WHERE tenant_id = ?` (or `WHERE organization_id = ?`) derived from the authenticated request context — wrap repositories in a `TenantScopedRepository` so callers cannot forget. BullMQ workers run independently and must inject the same scoped repository pattern. Cite `14-database-orm-patterns.md` and `33-multi-tenancy-patterns.md`.

Forward-only in production — never write a destructive `down`. Additive changes (new nullable column, new table, new index `CONCURRENTLY`) ship in one migration; backfills run as a separate job, not inline in the migration. Rename, drop, and column-type changes require a two-step (often three-step) rollout because old and new code must coexist during a rolling deploy. Rename `foo` → `bar`: (1) add `bar`, dual-write from app code (or a trigger) so both columns stay in sync, deploy the code that reads `bar` with a fallback to `foo`; (2) backfill `bar` from `foo`; (3) once all replicas run the new code, stop writing `foo`, drop it in a follow-up release. Type change is the same shape: add `amount_cents bigint`, dual-write, backfill, switch reads, drop the old column. Drops always wait one full release after the last reader is gone. Make tables/columns added with `ALTER TABLE` non-blocking — Postgres needs `ADD COLUMN ... NULL` (no default) to avoid a full table rewrite on large tables; add the default in a follow-up `UPDATE` batched. Use `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY`. Coordinate the deploy: migration runs before the new code, never after. Cite `15-migrations.md` and `16-cascade-rules.md`.

Use `ON DELETE CASCADE` because line items are owned data: `invoice_line_items.invoice_id` is part of their identity — a line item without an invoice is meaningless, and the parent's lifecycle defines the child's. The general rule is: if the parent owns the child (parent identity is part of the child's identity, child cannot exist independently), cascade. If the child references something it does not own — a shared lookup like `users` or `products` — use `RESTRICT` (or `NO ACTION`) so deleting the parent fails loudly until the references are dealt with explicitly; this prevents silent data loss on a user delete that would orphan invoices. If the reference is optional and the child should survive without it (e.g., `invoices.assigned_to_user_id`), use `SET NULL` — but only when the column is nullable and the business rule actually permits the null. Deviate when 'deletion' is logical: with soft deletes (`deleted_at`), CASCADE is rarely used; the parent flips its timestamp and reads filter children by joining on the parent's deleted state. Money/audit tables are a special case — never cascade delete invoices or payments at all; use soft delete plus retention policy. Cite `16-cascade-rules.md` and `13-database-design.md`.

Order is fixed by the framework: incoming request → middleware → Guard → Interceptor (pre-handler) → Pipe → Handler → Interceptor (post-handler) → exception Filter (only on throw). For this request: `JwtAuthGuard` runs first and decides if the caller is authenticated; if it returns false the request short-circuits with `401` and nothing else runs. Then `LoggingInterceptor` runs its pre-handler logic (start timer, log request) and wraps the rest in an RxJS pipeline. Then `ValidationPipe` transforms and validates the DTO — pipes run after guards because there is no point validating a body for an unauthenticated caller. Then the route handler executes the business call. After it resolves, the interceptor's `tap`/`map` runs (log latency, shape the response). Finally, if anything threw at any point, the global exception filter catches it and shapes `{ code, message, details?, traceId }`. The mental model: guards answer 'are you allowed in?', interceptors are cross-cutting wrappers (logging, caching, transforms), pipes are input transformers/validators, the handler is your business code, the filter is the single place errors become wire format. Cite `17-pipelines-interceptors-guards.md` and `39-exception-filters.md`.

Use EventEmitter2 here. Everything happens in-process inside one NestJS app, the listeners (billing trial setup, welcome email) are independent and tolerate failure, and there is no transactional handoff to another service. Pattern: `UsersService.create()` writes the user inside a DB transaction, and on commit emits `user.created` via EventEmitter2. `BillingService` and `EmailService` register `@OnEvent('user.created')` listeners; each one is independently retryable and a failure in one does not unwind the user creation. Do the emit after the transaction commits, never inside it — otherwise a rollback fires phantom events. Choose the outbox pattern when the event must cross a process boundary (queue, another service, a different DB) with at-least-once delivery semantics. Pattern: in the same DB transaction that creates the user, insert a row into an `outbox` table; a background worker (BullMQ) polls the outbox, publishes to Kafka/RabbitMQ/SNS, and marks the row delivered. This survives crashes between the DB write and the publish, which an in-memory emitter cannot. Quick rule: same process and best-effort → EventEmitter2. Crossing a network with delivery guarantees → outbox. Cite `18-events.md` and `19-background-jobs.md`.

Use BullMQ on Redis. Define an `llm-requests` queue and a `Processor` (`@Processor('llm-requests')`). The handler signature: `async process(job: Job<LlmJobData>) { ... }`. (1) Validate `job.data` against a Zod schema, fail fast to DLQ on bad shape. (2) Use `request_id` for idempotency — first thing the handler does is look up `llm_results` by `request_id`; if a row exists, return it without re-calling the LLM. (3) Call the LLM gateway (which itself does provider retry/fallback) with a per-call timeout. (4) Insert the result row and the usage row (`prompt_tokens`, `completion_tokens`, `cost`) inside one DB transaction keyed by `request_id` (unique constraint blocks double-writes if a retry races). (5) Return the result; BullMQ marks the job complete. Configuration: `attempts: 5`, `backoff: { type: 'exponential', delay: 1000 }`, `removeOnFail: false` so failed jobs persist; after the final attempt, BullMQ moves the job to its `failed` set — wire that up to a DLQ queue (`llm-requests-dlq`) via a `failed` listener for inspection and manual replay. Idempotency prevents double-charging users and duplicate writes; the DLQ prevents poison messages from looping forever and consuming Redis. Observability: per-job span, attempt count, and cost metric. Cite `19-background-jobs.md` and `22-observability.md`.

Load env once at boot through Nest's `ConfigModule.forRoot({ isGlobal: true, validate })` where `validate` is a Zod schema that parses `process.env` and throws on the first invalid value. The app crashes before binding the HTTP port — invalid env at boot is a small incident; invalid env discovered at runtime on a hot path is a customer outage. Schema example: `z.object({ NODE_ENV: z.enum(['development','staging','production']), PORT: z.coerce.number().int().positive(), DATABASE_URL: z.string().url(), JWT_SECRET: z.string().min(32) }).parse(process.env)`. Inject the parsed, typed config via a typed `AppConfigService`; never read `process.env` directly inside services. Loading env at runtime in a controller or service is dangerous because: (1) errors surface only when that path is hit, possibly in production weeks later; (2) different requests can see different values if env is mutated, breaking determinism and caching; (3) the value's validity is not tied to a single point, so logging/monitoring cannot prove the app is healthy on boot; (4) tests cannot assert configuration as a precondition. Fail fast on boot, validate with Zod, inject typed config. Secrets must come from env, never code or logs. Cite `20-configuration.md`.

Use `nestjs-pino` with JSON output in production and pretty output in development. Log: incoming request (method, path, status, latency, user_id if authed), outgoing response, errors with stack, slow DB queries, external API calls (provider, latency, status), background job lifecycle (start/finish/fail), and security-relevant events (login attempt, role change). Redact at the logger level so no surface can accidentally serialize secrets: `redact: { paths: ['req.headers.authorization', 'req.headers.cookie', 'res.headers["set-cookie"]', '*.password', '*.token', '*.apiKey'], remove: false, censor: '[REDACTED]' }`. Track a request across log lines with a correlation id: a middleware reads `x-request-id` from the inbound request (or generates a UUIDv7), attaches it to AsyncLocalStorage, and the pino logger includes it on every line as `correlation_id`. Pass it on outbound HTTP and into BullMQ job data so jobs and downstream services share the same id. JSON in prod is non-negotiable because plain-text logs cannot be queried at scale by Loki/Datadog/CloudWatch Insights. One unredacted Authorization header is a credential leak with a long tail — assume someone will grep your logs in five years. Cite `21-logging.md`.

Three layers, correlated by one id. (1) Traces with OpenTelemetry: auto-instrument HTTP, Postgres, Redis, and BullMQ; manually wrap the LLM call in a span carrying attributes for `llm.provider`, `llm.model`, `llm.prompt_tokens`, `llm.completion_tokens`, `llm.latency_ms`, `llm.cost_usd`, and `llm.request_id`. Child spans cover the HTTP call to the provider and the DB write that stores the result. Export to your OTel collector (Tempo/Jaeger/Honeycomb/Datadog APM). (2) LLM-specific tracing with Langfuse or Helicone: prompt input, completion output, tool calls, and per-call cost — the OTel trace shows the system path, Langfuse shows the LLM-quality path, and they share the same `request_id`. (3) Metrics: token count and cost as counters per `(user_id, org_id, model)`, latency as a histogram, error rate as a counter labeled by provider and error code; use these for dashboards and alerts. Structured logs carry the same `correlation_id` so a support ticket pivots into both traces. Quick mental model: traces show the timeline of one request, metrics show the aggregate health of all requests, logs show the details at one point. Cite `22-observability.md` and `26-ai-product-patterns.md`.

Unit test for the password-hashing service: instantiate the service with no Nest TestingModule (it is just a class), call `hash(password)` and assert the result starts with the bcrypt/argon2 prefix and verifies via `compare`. Use the real hashing library — do not mock `bcrypt`, you would just be re-asserting your mock. If the service depends on a clock or random, inject those and stub them. E2e test for the login endpoint: spin up the full Nest app via `Test.createTestingModule(...).createNestApplication()`, point it at a real test Postgres (Docker or testcontainers), seed a user with a known hash, hit `POST /auth/login` with supertest, assert `200` plus a session cookie or token, then assert `401` for the wrong password and `400` for a missing field — the test exercises the actual HTTP layer, ValidationPipe, guards, controller, service, and DB. Mock at boundaries — external APIs (Stripe, the LLM provider) get nock or a fake — but never mock the class under test, because that just re-asserts the mock and proves nothing about behavior. Service unit tests mock the repository (or use a real DB if logic is query-shaped) and assert behavior, not call counts. Cite `23-testing.md`.

N+1 happens when you fetch a list (`SELECT ... FROM invoices LIMIT 1000`) and then loop `forEach` to fetch each invoice's author and organization with a separate query — 1 + 1000 + 1000 + 1000 = 3001 queries, O(n) where it should be O(1). Prevent it by either issuing one JOIN that pulls all needed columns in a single round-trip (`SELECT i.*, a.name AS author_name, o.name AS org_name FROM invoices i JOIN users a ON ... JOIN organizations o ON ...`) or batch-loading: collect all `author_id`s, run one `WHERE id = ANY($1)` query, build an in-memory map, and stitch. ORMs help: Prisma `include`, TypeORM `relations`, Drizzle `with`. Use a DataLoader pattern when batching across async boundaries. Stream the response when the result set could be large enough to OOM the process or starve the event loop — anything over a few MB or a few thousand rows. Switch to `Transfer-Encoding: chunked` (`@Sse` or a manual `res.write` loop), iterate the DB cursor (`pg-cursor`, Prisma `findMany` with cursor pagination, or a server-side cursor), serialize each row as it arrives, and apply backpressure via the underlying stream so a slow client cannot fill memory. For analytics-style reports, prefer NDJSON or CSV over a giant JSON array. Cite `24-performance.md`.

Architecture: a single `LlmGateway` service exposes one interface (`stream`, `complete`, `embed`) and routes to provider adapters (`OpenAiAdapter`, `AnthropicAdapter`, `GeminiAdapter`) chosen by a policy: primary based on cost/latency/feature support, with declared fallbacks per capability. The gateway owns retry (exponential backoff with jitter, only on transient 5xx/429), per-provider timeouts, circuit breakers (open after N consecutive failures so a dead provider does not poison every request), and provider-aware rate-limit handling — read `x-ratelimit-remaining`/`Retry-After` and queue or shed accordingly. On a hard provider outage, the circuit opens and the next call falls back to the secondary; if all providers are down, return `503` with a typed `LLM_ALL_PROVIDERS_DOWN` error. Per-tenant rate limits live above the gateway in a Redis-backed token-bucket guard so noisy tenants cannot starve quiet ones. Usage tracking: every call (success or fail) writes a row into `llm_usage` with `{ user_id, org_id, provider, model, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, cost_usd, latency_ms, status }`; aggregations per user/org/model enforce monthly quotas — exceed → `429 QUOTA_EXCEEDED`. Observability: OTel spans wrap the gateway and each adapter; Langfuse receives full LLM traces with prompts, completions, and cost. Cite `26-ai-product-patterns.md`, `27-ai-streaming-sse.md`, `28-ai-usage-metering-cost.md`, and `22-observability.md`.

Logs: structured JSON per line with a `correlation_id` (UUIDv7 per request, propagated via AsyncLocalStorage), `user_id`, `org_id`, `invoice_id`, `operation` (`generate`, `validate`, `compute_tax`, `persist`, `notify`), `latency_ms`, and `outcome`. Errors include `error.code`, `error.message`, and stack — never the raw input. Traces: OpenTelemetry parent span for the request, child spans for each operation (`db.query`, `tax.compute`, `pdf.render`, `s3.put`, `email.send`) with attributes for `db.statement`, `external.url`, and durations; the trace shows the timeline so you can spot which step is slow without guessing. Metrics: `invoice_generate_latency_ms` histogram with `p50/p95/p99` per `org_id` and per `operation`, `invoice_generate_errors_total` counter labeled by error code, queue depth for any async work. Correlate by injecting `correlation_id` as both an OTel span attribute and a log field on every line — query Loki/Elastic by `correlation_id=<id>` to see all log lines, click into Tempo/Jaeger by the same id to see the trace timeline, and pivot to the metric dashboard scoped to that user. The mental model: traces show the timeline of one request, logs show the details at each point of that timeline, metrics show the aggregate health to compare this user's slow request against the population. Cite `21-logging.md` and `22-observability.md`.

Folder layout: `src/{core,common,modules,events,commands,integrations}` with one place for each kind of code — `core/` for app-wide infra (config, logger, database, cache), `common/` for generic utilities (pipes, decorators, types), `modules/` for business features (auth, billing, invoices, users), `integrations/` for external API clients (stripe, openai, s3), `events/` for domain event publishers/listeners, `commands/` for CLI/CRON entrypoints. Module breakdown follows bounded contexts: a module owns its tables and never reads another module's tables — it calls that module's service or subscribes to its events. Bootstrap order in `main.ts`/`AppModule`: (1) `ConfigModule.forRoot` with Zod validation, fail fast on bad env; (2) `LoggerModule` (nestjs-pino) so everything else logs structured; (3) Database module + run/verify migrations; (4) core cross-cutting (auth, cache, queue, observability); (5) feature modules; (6) `app.useGlobalPipes(new ValidationPipe(...))`, global exception filter, helmet, CORS whitelist, rate limiter, Swagger; (7) `app.enableShutdownHooks()`; (8) listen. Sanity checks: services instantiate without side effects, modules expose narrow public APIs via `exports`, no circular deps, no controller has business logic, every endpoint has a DTO. Avoid the classic mistake of importing across module internals — use `forwardRef` only as a smell flag, not a fix. Cite `01-folder-structure.md`, `03-module-design.md`, and `32-modern-nestjs-stack.md`.

Walk the code-review checklist for each of the five endpoints, marking pass/fail/NA: (1) DTO validation present and `whitelist: true` — block if any endpoint accepts `any` or skips ValidationPipe. (2) Error response shape consistent — every error returns `{ code, message, details?, traceId }` via the global exception filter; block on ad-hoc `{ success: false }` or naked strings. (3) HTTP status semantically correct — `400` validation, `401` auth, `403` authz, `404` not found, `409` conflict, `422` business-rule, `429` rate limit, `5xx` only on system errors; block on `200` for failure. (4) Security — no sensitive data in error messages or details (no stack traces, no DB query, no PII), redaction in logs verified. (5) Logging — every error path logs with `correlation_id`, stack at `error` level, no secrets. (6) Tests — at least one e2e per endpoint covering happy path and the new error paths; unit tests for any branching service logic. (7) Types — no `any` introduced; typed exception subclasses (`extends HttpException`). (8) Idempotency — money-moving or write endpoints accept `Idempotency-Key`; retried errors do not double-write. Blockers: missing DTO validation, leaked internals, wrong status, missing tests on new error paths. Suggestions (non-blocking): better error messages, naming polish, consolidating duplicate filters. Cite `29-code-review-checklist.md`, `10-error-handling.md`, and `39-exception-filters.md`.

(1) Business logic in the controller. Bad: controller hashes passwords and runs Prisma queries inline. Good: controller delegates `await this.usersService.create(dto)`; the service owns logic and the repository owns data access. (2) `@Body() body: any` with no DTO. Bad: trusts the client, no validation. Good: typed `CreateUserDto` with `@IsEmail`, `@IsString`, `@MinLength` etc., plus global `ValidationPipe({ whitelist: true, forbidNonWhitelisted: true, transform: true })`. (3) Hardcoded secrets. Bad: `const STRIPE_KEY = 'sk_live_...'` checked into source. Good: env-only via `ConfigModule` with Zod validation at boot, secrets injected via a typed `AppConfigService`, secret values redacted in logs. (4) N+1 query loops. Bad: `for (const i of invoices) i.author = await users.findById(i.authorId)`. Good: single `JOIN` or batch `WHERE id IN (...)` and stitch in memory; use `include`/`with`/DataLoader. (5) Mocking the class under test. Bad: in `UserService`'s test, `jest.spyOn(service, 'create').mockResolvedValue(...)` — that just re-asserts the mock. Good: instantiate the real service, mock its dependencies (repository, mailer) at the boundary, assert observable behavior. Each of these breaks tests, security, or the ability to evolve the system. Cite `30-code-review-anti-patterns.md`, `04`, `09`, `11`, `20`, `23`, `24`.

Tenant isolation: the user's tenant/org id is derived from the JWT or server-side session (`request.user.orgId`), never from the request body or URL — every device-record write filters `WHERE org_id = ? AND user_id = ?` derived from the authenticated context. Email validation: send the confirmation only to the user's verified email on file (`users.email_verified_at IS NOT NULL`); never to an address from the request. Rate limits: per-user cap on login attempts (e.g., 5/min, lockout after N) and per-user cap on confirmation emails sent (e.g., 3/hour) so an attacker cannot spam a victim or pummel an account; Redis-backed sliding window. Session handling: short-lived access tokens, rotating refresh tokens, hashed/HMACed session and refresh tokens server-side, secure httpOnly cookies with SameSite chosen for the topology (`lax`/`strict` same-site, `none; secure` true cross-site), and CSRF protection (double-submit token plus origin/fetch-metadata checks) on state-changing routes; revoke all sessions on password change or new-device confirmation rejection. Audit log: append-only `audit_events` row per login attempt with `{ user_id, org_id, ip, user_agent, device_fingerprint, outcome, timestamp, correlation_id }` plus the email send event; redact tokens/passwords; logs are write-once and queried by support during incidents. Cross-tenant leaks come from a missing `WHERE tenant_id = ?`, not from clever attacks — defense in depth at guard, service, and repository layers. Cite `11-security.md`, `12-authentication-patterns.md`, and `33-multi-tenancy-patterns.md`.

Priority 1: structured logging plus a stable error contract. Without correlated JSON logs and a `{ code, message, traceId }` shape, every incident is debugging-blind — you cannot pivot from a customer ticket to a stack trace. This unblocks every other refactor. Priority 2: pagination on list endpoints. Unpaginated lists are latent OOMs and DB outages; one customer with 200k rows takes the whole service down. Add cursor pagination behind a `ListQueryDto` with whitelisted filter/sort fields; ship the standard `{ data, meta }` envelope. Priority 3: DB indexes on FKs and hot query paths (`organization_id`, `created_at`, status filters). Latency improves, lock contention drops, and `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` shows the gains. Use `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` so the change is non-blocking. Priority 4: test coverage — add e2e tests on the endpoints you just touched and unit tests around any branching service logic, so the next refactor does not regress silently. Safe to leave for later: minor naming inconsistencies, comment cleanup, optional decorators, swagger polish, micro-optimizations not on a hot path. The order is principled: logging is the prerequisite for debugging; pagination prevents outages; indexes are steady-state hardening; tests lock in the gains. Do not bundle these — small surgical PRs per priority so a regression is bisectable. Cite `21`, `08`, `13`, `23`, and `00-execution-discipline.md`.

Signature verification: read the raw bytes — preserve `req.rawBody` on the webhook route (e.g. `NestFactory.create(AppModule, { rawBody: true })` or a route-scoped raw-body parser) before any JSON parsing happens. Use the algorithm the provider documents — HMAC-SHA256 is common (Stripe, GitHub `X-Hub-Signature-256`, Slack), but Ed25519 (Discord), HMAC-SHA1 (legacy), and JWT-style signatures also occur, so verify per provider against their current docs, do not assume. Compare with `crypto.timingSafeEqual(Buffer.from(a), Buffer.from(b))` after a length check (it throws on mismatched lengths). Reject with `401` (empty body, no detail) when the signature is missing, mismatched, or stale. Enforce a freshness window on the timestamp header (≤ 5 minutes typical) to block replay; rely on NTP for clock sync, do not widen the window to absorb skew. Do not apply the global `ValidationPipe` to webhook DTOs — the body is provider-shaped; Zod-parse the event after verification. Idempotency: store every event in a `webhook_events` table with a unique index on `(provider, event_id)` plus a `status` column (`received → processing → done | failed`). Insert-then-process: if the insert conflicts, return `2xx` without re-processing. Design downstream effects to be idempotent on the **domain entity** (e.g., a unique constraint on `payment_id`, not just `event_id`) since at-least-once providers sometimes deliver the same business event under different `event_id`s. Retention: prune old `webhook_events` rows on schedule (30–90 days, partitioned by `received_at`). Acknowledge fast: order is verify → insert dedupe row → enqueue → return `2xx`. The HTTP handler must not hold the connection while business logic runs — push it to a background worker (BullMQ) with a DLQ for failures. Always ack `2xx` for unhandled event types (event types you don't have a handler for); a `4xx` for an 'unknown' type causes infinite provider retries. Multi-tenant routing: resolve the tenant id from the verified payload (mapped from the provider account id stored at integration setup), never from URL or query; use per-tenant secrets where the provider supports them; rehydrate tenant context in the worker before business code runs. Never trust the body for high-stakes state (payments, refunds, account changes) — re-fetch authoritative state from the provider's API by id, then act. Logging: log `provider`, `event_id`, `event_type`, `tenant_id`, `received_at`, `result`, `latency_ms`, `correlation_id`. Never log the raw body, signature header, or auth header — they may carry PII, card metadata, or signed URLs that count as secrets; configure redaction in the logger. Cite `36-webhooks.md`, `11-security.md`, `19-background-jobs.md`, `21-logging.md`, `33-multi-tenancy-patterns.md`.

Split modernization from feature work to isolate risk. Release A (modernization, no behavior changes): bump Node to the new LTS in Dockerfile, CI, and `engines`; bump NestJS major from v17 to v19 with the official codemods; resolve breaking changes in module/decorator APIs; run the full unit + e2e suite; deploy to staging and bake — this PR adds zero features, so any regression is the framework upgrade. Release B onward (TypeORM modernization, phased): (1) add new patterns alongside old — introduce a thin repository abstraction so old query-builder code and new patterns coexist; (2) migrate high-risk flows first (payments, idempotent writes) behind feature flags, with strong test coverage and metrics dashboards comparing latency and error rate before/after; (3) migrate medium-risk flows; (4) retire old patterns and remove the abstraction shim. Pre-conditions before any of this: test coverage on the payment service must be high enough that a green build means the feature works — otherwise you are flying blind; add missing e2e first. Never mix a Node bump, a Nest bump, and a TypeORM rewrite in one PR — you cannot bisect. Validate volatile facts (current LTS, current Nest major, codemod availability) against official docs and the repo's lockfile, not memory. Cite `35-source-of-truth-freshness.md`, `32-modern-nestjs-stack.md`, `00-execution-discipline.md`, and `23-testing.md`.

Critical bug: a client-supplied `user_id` from the request body is server-trusted, so any caller can act as any user — full account takeover, full cross-tenant data access. This is the core anti-pattern multi-tenant systems must eliminate; it is not a small bug. Fix: never accept `user_id` from the client. The `user_id` (and `org_id`/`tenant_id`) is derived from the authenticated session — extracted from the verified JWT claims by a guard (`JwtAuthGuard`) and attached to the request as `request.user`. From there, services, repositories, and queries pull `request.user.id` and `request.user.orgId` via a `@CurrentUser()` parameter decorator or AsyncLocalStorage; the body DTO does not even have a `userId` field, and if it did, `whitelist: true` would strip it. Defense in depth: guards authenticate and attach context; services pass the trusted ids explicitly to repositories; repositories include `WHERE user_id = ? AND org_id = ?` in every query. The rule is: server-derived identity, never client-supplied. Add an integration test that calls the endpoint with an authenticated user A and a body containing `user_id: B` — it must succeed as A and ignore the spoof. Audit the codebase for any handler that pulls an actor id from `@Body()` or `@Param()` without an ownership check. Cite `33-multi-tenancy-patterns.md` and `12-authentication-patterns.md`.

Use a Redis-backed sliding-window or token-bucket limiter — not in-memory, because every server in the fleet must agree on the count for one user. Limits are per `(user_id, tier)` keyed in Redis: e.g., free 60/min, pro 600/min, enterprise 6000/min, plus a global per-IP floor to soak up unauth abuse. A `RateLimitGuard` reads `request.user.id` and `request.user.tier`, checks/decrements the bucket atomically (Lua or `INCR` + `EXPIRE`), and either lets the request through or rejects. On exceed return HTTP `429 Too Many Requests` with `Retry-After: <seconds>` and a body of `{ code: 'RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED', message, details: { tier, limit, remaining: 0, resetAt }, traceId }`. Also surface `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, and `X-RateLimit-Reset` headers on every response so well-behaved clients self-regulate. For graduated enforcement: a soft limit at 80% logs a warning event (`rate_limit.near`), a hard limit at 100% blocks. Distributed correctness: centralized Redis cluster, not per-pod state; expire keys correctly so users do not stay locked out beyond the window. Logging: log near-limit and exceeded events per user with `correlation_id` so dashboards can show which tenants are hot. Tier changes (upgrade/downgrade) flush the bucket. Cite `11-security.md`, `20-configuration.md`, and `22-observability.md`.

Structure the handler around three principles: idempotency, classify errors, and observe everything. Step one: validate the job payload with a Zod schema and fail fast — bad shape goes straight to the DLQ, not into a retry loop. Step two: idempotency check — look up `invoice_results` by the job's `request_id` (or `invoice_id` + idempotency key); if a result exists, return it without re-running. Step three: do the work inside a transaction, write the result and the audit row keyed by `request_id` with a unique constraint so a racing retry cannot double-insert. Step four: classify errors. Transient (network blip, 5xx from a dependency, deadlock): throw — BullMQ retries with exponential backoff (`attempts: 5`, `backoff: { type: 'exponential', delay: 1000, jitter: true }`). Permanent (validation, missing user, business-rule violation, security error): mark the job as failed without retry by throwing an `UnrecoverableError` so it goes straight to the DLQ. Once `attempts` is exhausted, BullMQ moves the job to `failed`; a `failed` listener republishes to a DLQ queue for manual inspection and replay. Logging on every attempt: `attempt`, `error.code`, `error.message`, `correlation_id`, `request_id`, `latency_ms`. Metrics: per-job duration histogram, retry counter, success/failure rate, DLQ depth gauge. Cite `19-background-jobs.md` and `22-observability.md`.

Module-boundary violation: one module owns its tables, and `BillingService` has reached across into the `users` module's repository to run raw SQL on `users`. That breaks the non-negotiable that a module owns its data — every future schema change in `users` (rename `email`, add a constraint, switch to soft delete, partition the table) silently breaks billing, and the dependency is invisible because it is hidden in a SQL string instead of an import graph. Right fix: `BillingModule` imports `UsersModule`, and `UsersModule` exports a public `UsersService` with a narrow `getEmailById(userId: string): Promise<string>` (or `getContactSnapshot`) method. Billing calls `usersService.getEmailById(userId)`. Now the contract is a typed service method that the users team can evolve without breaking billing — versioning, caching, and access checks all live behind that method. Alternative when billing only needs a denormalized snapshot for receipts and does not need fresh reads on every send: subscribe to a `user.created` / `user.email_changed` event from `users`, project the email into `billing.receipt_contacts`, and read from billing's own table. The DB stops being a shared API and goes back to being module-internal. Cite `03-module-design.md`, `18-events.md`, and the non-negotiable that one module owns its tables.

Every name violates the convention. `class userservice` should be `UserService` — classes are PascalCase. The file `UserService.ts` should be `user.service.ts` — files are kebab-case with a `.kind` suffix (`.service.ts`, `.controller.ts`, `.module.ts`, `.dto.ts`, `.pipe.ts`, `.guard.ts`). `interface IUserProfile` should be `UserProfile` — drop the Hungarian `I` prefix; TypeScript interfaces and types are not C#. `type TPaymentRow` should be `PaymentRow` — same reason, no Hungarian `T` prefix. Env var `databaseUrl` should be `DATABASE_URL` — env vars are SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE. Constant `maxRetries = 3` should be `MAX_RETRIES` — module-level true constants are SCREAMING_SNAKE; locals would stay camelCase, but a `const` representing config is SCREAMING_SNAKE. File `createUserDTO.ts` should be `create-user.dto.ts` — kebab-case file plus the `.dto.ts` kind suffix; the symbol inside is `CreateUserDto` (PascalCase, `Dto` not `DTO`). Folder `Pipes/` should be `pipes/` — folders are lowercase plural for utility/kind buckets. File `ParseCuid.Pipe.ts` should be `parse-cuid.pipe.ts` — kebab-case plus `.pipe.ts`. Variables stay camelCase, classes/interfaces/types PascalCase, files kebab-case + `.kind`, env SCREAMING_SNAKE, DB columns snake_case. Cite `02-naming-conventions.md`.

URLs are plural nouns under URI versioning `/v1/payments`. `GET /v1/payments` lists with cursor pagination → `200` with `{ data, meta }`. `POST /v1/payments` charges a card → `201 Created` with a `Location: /v1/payments/:id` header and the created resource; MUST require `Idempotency-Key` because it has money side-effects — same key in the next 24h returns the same resource without re-charging. `PUT /v1/payments/:id` full replace → `200` (or `204` if no body) and is idempotent by definition. `PATCH /v1/payments/:id` partial update → `200`; PATCH is not idempotent in general (a partial change applied twice can produce different states), so accept `Idempotency-Key` for any change with side-effects. `POST /v1/payments/:id/refund` is a sub-resource action — refunds are events, not field edits, so they get their own URL and a `POST` → `201` with the refund object; require `Idempotency-Key` because double-refunds are the exact failure to prevent. `DELETE /v1/payments/:id` → `204 No Content`, idempotent (deleting an already-deleted resource still returns `204` or `404` consistently). Versioning: bump `v1`→`v2` only on breaking changes (removed/renamed fields, changed semantics); additive fields stay in `v1`. Errors follow the standard `{ code, message, details?, traceId }` shape. Cite `06-api-design.md` and `07-standard-responses.md`.

Multiple violations. (1) `private repo = new InvoiceRepository()` constructs the dependency itself instead of receiving it via constructor DI — the service cannot be tested without booting the real repository, the lifecycle is not managed by Nest, and you lose interception/decoration. Fix: `constructor(private readonly repo: InvoiceRepository) {}` and let the IoC container inject. (2) `input: any` and `Promise<any>` throw away type safety — there is no contract for callers, no IDE help, and the DTO/return shape can drift. Fix: typed `ProcessInvoiceInput` DTO (validated by class-validator) and an `Invoice` return type. (3) The 180-line method violates single-responsibility — validation, payment, persistence, notification, audit logging, and provider-fallback are six distinct responsibilities mashed into one orchestration. Each gets its own collaborator: `InvoiceValidator`, `PaymentGateway`, `InvoiceRepository`, `Mailer`, `AuditLogger`. The public `process()` becomes a thin coordinator that calls each in sequence and is easy to read at a glance. (4) Provider-fallback (retry, secondary provider) belongs behind a `PaymentGateway` abstraction — that is exactly the gateway pattern's job. The service should not know there are two providers. Refactor: inject all collaborators via constructor, replace `any` with typed DTO + return type, split into small private methods (validate, charge, persist, notify, audit) orchestrated by `process`, and push provider concerns into the gateway. Cite `04-code-quality.md` and `26-ai-product-patterns.md`.

(1) RBAC alone is not enough. A role check answers "is this kind of user allowed to call this kind of endpoint at all" — both editors A and B pass `role === 'editor'`, so RBAC happily lets editor A read editor B's document. That is IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference): a valid role plus a guessable `:id` equals data leakage. You also need an object-level authorization check that the document actually belongs to the requester, or to a tenant/org the requester belongs to. (2) Layer the checks. JwtAuthGuard runs first, verifies the token, and attaches `request.user = { id, orgId, role }` — that is authentication, the "who." A `RolesGuard` (or `@Roles('editor')` decorator) enforces the coarse role — that is the "what kind of action." Then ownership/tenant scoping lives in the service and repository: the query must include `WHERE id = :id AND owner_id = :userId` (or `AND tenant_id = :tenantId`), with `userId`/`tenantId` derived from the authenticated `request.user` — never from the URL or body. The repository is the last line because it is the layer closest to the data; defense in depth means a bug in the controller or service still cannot leak across users. Tests must prove user A cannot read user B's document even with a valid JWT and the right role. Cite `11-security.md`, `12-authentication-patterns.md`, `33-multi-tenancy-patterns.md`, and the non-negotiable that every URL id is checked against the authenticated user's ownership.
